comment_text:
The director did an incredible job of weaving an underlying message throughout Season 1 — showing how truly happy the main character was when he was surrounded by his mother, daughter, friends, and even the joy of reuniting with his childhood companion. He was always smiling, even in bad situations, because his life was filled with people he loved.
 
But when he lost all of them, and finally obtained what he had been chasing all along — money — he came to understand the true value of life. Unfortunately, that realisation came too late. After manipulating the old man in their final game and winning, it became clear that the prize was hollow; the cost had been too high. Money, in the end, could not replace the warmth, love, and connection he had lost.
 
The ending serves as a tragic reminder: in the pursuit of wealth, he sacrificed everything that truly mattered. And so, burdened by regret, he met his fate — not in the games, but in life itself — proving that the richest person is not the one with the most money, but the one with the most to live for.

comment_text: I have an idea. If you want to revive GiHun. It could be (part of the season 3 or all of it) GiHun or someone else's nightmare so he didn't die for real and can reappear in a new season. Or what if it was a psychological trick to test the players or even crush them even more (would be crueler but the creator and the VIPs are psychos). And in the new season is introduced a character that grew up as a pariah but still has kindness and humanity. And all notice that looks like GiHun, leaving all shocked even GiHun. Not only that, he came looking for answers because one of his friends disappeared a year ago. And his last words on the phone were I guy in a suit gave me the opportunity of a life time to become rich. So since then he looks for the guy in a suit. Randomly he finds him and gets a proposition he agrees but before calling the recruiter he tells the police he wants to be a mole so he uncovers the truthbehind his friends whereabouts. When joining he tells the truth as his backstory he grew up rejected and excluded by his peers a total pariah. But he joins to help his parents because he earns minimum wage and his parents have chronic aliments, he joins despite having chronic limping and near sight thus wears glasses (the son and GiHun don'tknow about each other, he the son, is adopted with zero info about his birth parents). I have another idea a 13th game because the director likes symbolisms and numerology. This would be musical: play Mozart's Rondo alla Turca or Bethoveen moonlight sonata 3rd movement..or guess the note or the song by ear. Or guess the composer. Many would die in seconds. Yikes.

🎬 In-Movie Example Style:

	Just as Gi-hun leaps, a trapdoor opens, and he falls into a concealed safety chamber. Cameras broadcast his “death” to the audience and the VIPs, but underground rebels save him. The baby wins, the public is manipulated — but Gi-hun lives to dismantle the system from the shadows.

💥 Why It Would Work:

	•	It would shock viewers, rekindle momentum for the franchise, and open the door for a final showdown series or movie.
	•	Thematically, it fits: Squid Game explores deception, survival, and human resilience.

comment_text: Most people miss all the hidden biblical allegory the writer secretly slipped into the entire show. Player 001 can be referred to as Lucifer (the one seated at the top who rules over the pyramid) who represents the first and player 456 (as jesus who returns again to help save people trapped in lucifers bondage by helping them come outve bondage or the system of greed, debt and temptation) and who represents the last. Lucifer comes down from his high places of rule and enters into the game to balance the scales and work of jesus. They both are on a mission (riding white horses). One seeks to prove how humans are inferior, sinful and selfish... and deserve to suffer. The other seeks to prove how humans can repent and become better people, and who can become selfless by sacrificing themselves for others and therefor deserve to be forgiven and passed over in wrath. One hides behind a mask (as does his minions) representing his power as the "covering" cherub that opperates in secret through his occult. The other comes and brings truth about the system, what it is and how to come outve it by following him. He never gives up hope for humanity even though he comes close several times because so many are lost in their own greed and sin. Yet he stands up for the pure of heart and fights for them. At every turn, the front man (or first player) fights against him mentally to undo all of the work he does. Its a spiritual battle between the one who believes in the small remnant pure of heart vs the one who has lost faith in humanity and now believes in the larger group of evil people. The front man was once good and lost his humanity by having his heart (or morality) become corrupted by his logic and reasoning. He's very clever at weighing options between two dual paths (tree of knowledge of good and evil) but is terrible at using his love to find the 3rd option from the tree of life. Theres so much more hidden throughout the show that everyone misses. But you get the picture.

comment_text: To those who hated the ending… i found someone comment this so im sharing.. This made me realize Gi-hun's final act wasn't just a rejection of the game. It was a rejection of the philosophy that created it. The Front Man believed humans are inherently selfish, driven by survival and greed, no better than horses forced to run for others’ amusement. But Gi-hun proved that wrong by doing the one thing no one else could refusing to play, even if it meant dying. His choice wasn’t strategic. It wasn’t for reward. It was to preserve something bigger than himself, something like compassion, dignity, and choice. His last words, "We are not horses. We are humans. Humans are..." The silence afterward says everything. It’s not unfinished by accident. It’s a question, a challenge, maybe even a warning. How do you define human after everything we’ve seen? The games showed us the worst. Betrayal. Cruelty. Cowardice. But they also showed us sacrifice, love, and resistance. So what are humans? Are we good? Evil? Selfish? Kind? Maybe we’re all of it at once. Gi-hun’s story doesn’t give us a clean answer. It forces you to ask what being human even means. Is humanity just compassion? If so, what about the people who created the games? They were human too. The word humanity starts to lose meaning when you realize the same species capable of kindness is also capable of horror. In the end, Gi-hun didn’t try to fix humanity. He just chose to be the kind of human he believed should exist. Not perfect. Not heroic. Just human. And maybe that’s the only answer there is.

comment_text: Brilliant acting (apart from the VIPs I must say), and important messages conveyed as always, especially through that last line of Gi-hun. However, I am quite disappointed with the lack of development for this season. I don’t hate it, but I do think more could have been done with certain  things that were built up in season 2. For example, Dae-ho’s character had a lot of potential to be a character that highlights someone trying to surpass the challenges, specifically Korean, society throws at you, his struggles in the military etc, however that was discarded. I suppose that is to emphasise how humans are always lying, making up things to put themselves in a positive light, but I wish it wasn’t Dae-ho it was done to. Additionally, all the rule bending. Normally, you wouldn’t be allowed to switch teams, know what the game is, or force someone to be a player (baby), it all doesn’t seem very natural. There are many other things I could point out, but overall no I am not disappointed with this season because it’s “sad” and isn’t a super happy ending, I am disappointed because there were so many interesting ideas that were built up in season 2 left completely untouched. 6/10.

comment_text: This season was very dumb I really disliked it. So many dumb little things that annoyed me. Like that trans soldier, how she died. She's a soldier she should walk up the room and close the door behind her. You would think that a soldier would knew to protect its back. Or the moment I saw that one bomb with big ass screen showing time to blow up, and they even added the speaker just to beep. Very childish. And everything with ectasy. It's not how it works, at all. I know that in Korea drugs are the biggest evil and they don't know anything about them, but as an author, You would think to just do quick Google search of the effects. Ectasy doesn't work like that, no hallucinations. And while I can let it slide, explaining them not by the pills but his mental state, I can't do the same with his supposed withdraws. Ectasy withdraws are not like that. How many pills did he ate? 2 of them? And then 2 days later he still has strong withdraws like he took brown. Ectasy doesn't result in such withdraws, especially when he ate only 2. He would feel a little bit worse, less energetic, for a day and that's all. Very dumb. Also the moment they said that the child is considered a player I said to myself that OK, it's obvious he's gonna be the winner. Lastly, I really liked the character of that guy who died the last before Gi hun, until the last episode. He was willing to do what is needed to protect himself, he's child and he's girl. Bewertung: ★☆☆☆☆

Text:
Die letzte Staffel von Squid Game ist ein absolutes Desaster. Völlig sinnlose Story, peinlich schlechte Dialoge und null Spannung. Es wirkt, als wäre den Machern alles egal gewesen – Hauptsache, schnell irgendwas zusammenschustern, um noch Geld zu kassieren. Die Figuren benehmen sich unlogisch, jede Folge zieht sich wie Kaugummi, und das Finale ist einfach nur ein Witz. Ich habe mich selten so gelangweilt und gleichzeitig geärgert, meine Zeit damit verschwendet zu haben. Netflix sollte sich schämen, so einen lieblosen Müll zu veröffentlichen. Absolut nicht empfehlenswert.
